question.text: Mr. Sharma, a resident Indian, wants to invest Rs. 30,000 in a mutual fund scheme of ABC Mutual Fund. He does not have a PAN card. He has already invested Rs. 25,000 across other schemes of the same fund house in the last 8 months. Which statement is correct regarding PAN exemption?

answer.text: He is NOT eligible for PAN exemption as his aggregate investment across all schemes of the fund house would exceed Rs. 50,000 in a rolling 12-month period
He is eligible for PAN exemption since each individual investment is below Rs. 50,000
He is eligible for PAN exemption as the Rs. 50,000 limit applies per scheme, not per fund house
He is NOT eligible because PAN exemption is only available for SIP investments, not lump sum

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** PAN exemption for micro investments applies only when the aggregate investment across ALL schemes of a fund house is up to Rs. 50,000 in a rolling 12-month period. Mr. Sharma's total would be Rs. 55,000 (Rs. 25,000 + Rs. 30,000), exceeding the Rs. 50,000 threshold.
**The Trap:** The Rs. 50,000 limit is aggregate across all schemes of the fund house, not per scheme. It applies on a rolling 12-month basis, not per financial year. The exemption applies to both lump sum and SIP — the mode of investment is irrelevant.
**Cross-Exam Context:** PAN exemption thresholds for micro investments are tested in NISM Series V-A and X-A. The aggregate calculation across schemes is a common trick question.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always check the client's total investment history across all schemes of the same AMC before processing a PAN-exempt transaction. Maintain a running total on a rolling 12-month basis.
